dba56f73-ca9f-4d45-8834-ddcd6b787d61.gsolenFeedback:
Desde mi punto de vista, GSoLEN representa una iniciativa especialmente valiosa por su enfoque explícito en la ciencia del aprendizaje como base para la mejora de las políticas y las prácticas educativas, y por su compromiso con la reducción de las desigualdades en el aprendizaje, temática que me preocupa especialmente. La articulación entre investigación, práctica profesional y toma de decisiones educativas es un desafío central a nivel global, y los objetivos de la red dialogan de manera directa con mis intereses académicos y profesionales.
 
 
 
 GSoLEN podría enriquecer mi investigación al facilitar el intercambio con especialistas de distintos contextos y sistemas educativos, así como el acceso a discusiones comparadas sobre políticas educativas basadas en evidencia. Asimismo, la red podría potenciar la transferencia de conocimiento hacia la formación docente y el diseño de intervenciones educativas informadas por la ciencia.
 
 
 
 Al mismo tiempo, creo que puedo aportar a GSoLEN desde la investigación aplicada, la divulgación científica y la capacitación docente, especialmente en contextos de diversidad y desigualdad educativa. Mi experiencia en el desarrollo de materiales, evaluaciones e intervenciones en comprensión lectora, así como en la traducción del conocimiento científico para públicos no especializados, puede contribuir a fortalecer el impacto social y educativo de la red.

19e9512d-8026-4c01-a7ba-d5b0f2bdd00e.gsolenFeedback:
I see GSoLEN as one of the few spaces globally where learning science, practice, and systems-level experimentation are held together with intellectual rigor and humility. My recent engagement through the UNESCO conference, including opening a panel on trauma-informed learning through entrepreneurial education, reinforced for me how valuable this network can be in bridging evidence, lived experience, and implementation. The conversations and continued dialogues with Stephan Leeds and Kelly Shiohira around potential partnerships have been especially generative, with action steps currently taking shape.
 
 
 
 My work spans corporate L&D, early-stage (0→1) startups, and entrepreneurial mindset and product-studio-based learning programs that I have designed, developed, and scaled through multi-sector partnerships across India, Europe, and the United States (offline and digital). Earlier this year, while working in the international development sector, I was impacted by the U.S. withdrawal from UNESCO and subsequent furloughs. Since then, I have been intentionally transitioning into higher education and innovation-focused ecosystems, and I currently work with Cornell Tech as an MBA Coach supporting entrepreneurial and interdisciplinary learning.
 
 
 
 Looking ahead, I am particularly interested in deeper engagement with organizations and leaders working at the intersection of education innovation, entrepreneurship, and workforce development, especially within higher education and EdTech spaces. I would value GSoLEN’s support in connecting with credible people, initiatives, and longer-term, values-aligned opportunities based on the U.S. East Coast (including New York and Philadelphia) where learning science is actively translated into practice.
 
 
 
 Since I also mentioned that I was born in India, I want to address an increasingly important question in the U.S. -- I hold full work authorization in the United States and am eager to contribute meaningfully to research-practice partnerships, pilots, and system-level conversations that move beyond one-off experiments toward durable change.

1c351e54-5f6b-4c64-8d2d-6727acb51016.gsolenFeedback:
In 2013 I completed my PhD under the supervision of Professors Pamela Heaton and Elisabeth Hill exploring music and language cognition in children with Specific Language Impairment. After finishing my PhD, I worked for a short time as a postdoctoral researcher working with Professor Lauren Stewart on a project investigating socio-emotional cognition in adults with a congenital music disorder ('Amusia'). I then spent 10-years leading the psychology department at a secondary school in Reading, developing the curriculum and teaching A-levels. During this time, I worked with the Head and Governing Body to help to establish and lead the operations of the charitable company 'BrainCanDo' to support teachers and practitioners to engage with research and further an evidence-informed approach to educational policy and practice.
 
 
 
 I continue to retain my interest in how we can support practitioners to engage with research in order to drive improvement in educational policy and practice and would find it incredibly valuable to be part of an active network of like-minded researchers and practitioners so that together we can continue to explore effective means of knowledge-brokering and global partnership.

eaa2b4b6-3ad7-4c7f-95a8-eef7a4eaa1d7.gsolenFeedback:
I am excited about the potential of the GSoLEN. In an era where generative AI and immersive technologies are rapidly reshaping how students learn chemistry and science, a global network grounded in evidence-based learning sciences is exactly what the education community needs.
 
 
 
 My own work from developing VR/AR chemistry labs, publishing on cognitive diagnostic models in chemistry assessments, exploring Universal Design for Learning (UDL) in lab teaching, to examining how over-reliance on Gen-AI can create a learning fracture that weakens resilience and sustainable thinking – sits at the intersection of chemistry education, EdTech, and the science of learning. 
 
 
 
 I have also been actively involved in science diplomacy and chemical security education across ASEAN and IUPAC frameworks, always with the belief that rigorous learning science must inform both classroom practice and policy.GSoLEN could support my current and future work in several concrete ways: Provide a trusted platform to run multinational studies on how extended-reality tools and AI scaffolding affect skill mastery and metacognition in chemistry and sustainability education.
 
 
 
 Amplify voices from smaller institutions and the Global South in setting the global science-of-learning agenda, something I tried to do through IUPAC, IYCN, and GYA projects.
 
 
 
 Offer seed funding or matchmaking for interdisciplinary proposals that blend neuroscience, chemistry education, and science diplomacy – areas I am increasingly merging.
 
 
 
 In return, I would be eager to contribute experience in teacher professional development.

To support my work, the network could be most valuable in a few concrete ways:
Collaboration: connecting me with policymakers, practitioners, and researchers working on similar implementation challenges and on possible project development aligned to the network’s convening role. Applied cascading: providing and sharing information and peer feedback to help translate evidence from diverse educational settings into usable formats (policy briefs, teacher-training tools, implementation cases), aligned with the planned UNESCO–GSoLEN framework. Empowering and implementation learning: short-term workshops, meetings, or practice communities that help members identify institutional gaps, build human-resource capability for evidence use, and adapt interventions to context. EdTech evidence and quality: building upon the standards for digital learning, especially given the stated need to strengthen evidence on effectiveness and quality in technology-enhanced learning environments
